caveats: a: The occupational source is four-digit and not specific to engine equipment.
a: Regulatory complexity increases documentation exposure but also requires expert accountability that limits substitution.
rho: No representative 333618 adoption or realized-savings dataset was found.
rho: Large global engine OEMs may be more digitized than the LMM population, while small specialty firms may have cleaner but smaller datasets.
e: The injected n of 73 is an ESTIMATE based on receipts and a machinery-sector margin rather than observed 333618 EBITDA.

s5: A small number of specialty-engine transactions could materially alter the measured rate.

q: Retention likely differs between proprietary specialty engines and customer-controlled platform designs.
d5: The BLS projection combines engines with turbines and power-transmission equipment.
d5: EIA and EPA establish applications and constraints but do not measure the LMM industry's forward demand.

o: Automation can reduce labor intensity without eliminating the accountable operating and certifying firm.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business engine and parts sales under OEM programs, dealer or distributor channels, fleet specifications, generator and equipment platforms, and replacement or service-parts relationships, generally priced by unit, configuration, or supply agreement.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive plants, non-control investment vehicles, automotive gasoline engines, aircraft engines, pure distributors, engine rebuilders without manufacturing, and equipment assemblers that purchase rather than manufacture the engine are excluded.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers of internal-combustion engines other than automotive gasoline and aircraft engines, including diesel, semidiesel, stationary, marine, nonroad, and other application engines supplied repeatedly to external OEM, distributor, fleet, power, and industrial customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for four-digit 333600 rather than wage-weighted task exposure in 333618. I map engineering analysis, calibration support, software and controls work, compliance documentation, quoting, planning, procurement, quality analysis, and portions of CNC monitoring and inspection to exposure, while discounting machining, assembly, test-cell operation, maintenance, logistics, and physical validation; turbine and power-transmission segments create uncertain net bias.
caveats: The occupational source is four-digit and not specific to engine equipment.
Regulatory complexity increases documentation exposure but also requires expert accountability that limits substitution.
a.change_evidence: A current wage-weighted task census at independent 333618 firms, covering design, simulation, calibration, emissions compliance, software, quoting, CNC, assembly, testing, quality, supply chain, sales, and administration, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: In 333600, production occupations represented 47.86% of 2023 employment, engineering 17.03%, management 7.84%, business and financial operations 6.23%, and computer and mathematical work 2.38%. Engine makers have substantial design, test, diagnostics, emissions-certification, software, documentation, and planning work exposed to AI, but accountable physical build and test labor remains large.

d5.bridge: BLS projects output for all 333600 rather than 333618 constant-quality demand; EIA and EPA document uses and regulatory scope rather than forward volumes. I start below the broad industry's roughly 1.07 five-year growth implication because electrification and emissions compliance can remove engine demand, then retain upside for backup power, construction, remote power, industrial, marine, and fuel-flexible applications; segment error is bidirectional.
caveats: The BLS projection combines engines with turbines and power-transmission equipment.
EIA and EPA establish applications and constraints but do not measure the LMM industry's forward demand.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year unit and constant-dollar forecasts by stationary, generator, marine, construction, agricultural, recreational, and other engine application, reconciled to electrification rates, EPA certificates, imports, announced platforms, and replacement demand, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: BLS projects four-digit output growth of 1.3% annually, while EIA documents widespread internal-combustion use for construction-site mobile power and emergency or backup generation. EPA regulates diverse nonroad heavy equipment, marine, recreational, and small-equipment engines, confirming a broad application base but also compliance pressure. Electrification, batteries, fuel cells, emissions rules, and platform redesign create substantial downside.

caveats: The injected n of 73 is an ESTIMATE based on receipts and a machinery-sector margin rather than observed 333618 EBITDA.
Firm and establishment boundaries are particularly problematic for diversified engine and equipment OEMs.
e.change_evidence: A plant-level ownership and activity map from D&B Hoovers, Power Systems Research, CapIQ, EPA certificates of conformity, company filings, permits, customer recurrence, and audited normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The code matches repeat external engine supply, but much engine production sits inside large diversified OEMs, integrated equipment groups, or captive plants. The receipts-based LMM bridge can misclassify capital-intensive manufacturers, and some small businesses are rebuilders or equipment assemblers rather than transferable engine manufacturers.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 333618 ($5,070,254,655) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 333618 ($29,813,218,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $724,413 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 333618); class <5: 60 firms, avg revenue/firm $995,950 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $195,405 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 26 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,481,192 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $486,810 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,631,276 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,104,856 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,599,432 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,060,609 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $91,300,304 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,913,120 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 43 firms, avg revenue/firm $621,845,605 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $122,006,108 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 73

caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than an observed share.
Automation can reduce labor intensity without eliminating the accountable operating and certifying firm.
o.change_evidence: Commercial lights-out engine-line benchmarks, autonomous test and calibration releases, regulatory acceptance of AI-generated certification evidence, and staffing changes after robotics and AI deployments would materially move this value.
o.rationale: Any year-five demand remaining for the current engine basket still requires an accountable manufacturer to design, machine, assemble, calibrate, test, certify, warrant, and support physical engines. Electrification and alternative powertrain displacement reduce d5; they do not convert surviving engine quantity into software or customer self-service.

caveats: No representative contract-level pass-through data were available.
Retention likely differs between proprietary specialty engines and customer-controlled platform designs.
q.change_evidence: OEM and distributor contracts, productivity clauses, platform terms, certification ownership, warranty and service-parts economics, renewal cadence, contribution margins, and price response after documented cost improvements would materially move retention.
q.rationale: Engine qualification, controls and emissions integration, installed platforms, service parts, durability evidence, dealer support, and switching cost can preserve benefits. Large OEM customers, fleets and distributors negotiate aggressively, demand annual productivity, and can dual-source or redesign platforms, passing part of savings into price.

rho.bridge: NIST's augmented-intelligence program addresses machine-tool health and process prediction across manufacturing, not implementation by LMM engine makers. I transfer feasibility to machining and test operations, then discount plant-specific data, mixed legacy assets, safety and emissions validation, cyber risk, model traceability, certification, and capital constraints; research prototypes likely bias readiness upward.
caveats: No representative 333618 adoption or realized-savings dataset was found.
Large global engine OEMs may be more digitized than the LMM population, while small specialty firms may have cleaner but smaller datasets.
rho.change_evidence: A survey of engine manufacturers on PLM, simulation, test-data architecture, ERP/MES connectivity, AI pilots, regulatory validation, exception rates, implementation cost, and verified labor hours avoided by workflow would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: Engineering search, specification review, software assistance, calibration analysis, quoting, scheduling, supply-chain work, inspection triage, predictive maintenance, and compliance-document drafting can be implemented within five years. Engine safety, durability and emissions certification, sparse failures, physical tests, and regulated sign-off keep experts and operators in the loop.

caveats: Neither the qualifying transfer numerator nor eligible denominator is observed.
A small number of specialty-engine transactions could materially alter the measured rate.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year transaction and certificate-ownership history from PitchBook, CapIQ, EPA certification records, Power Systems Research, corporate filings, and plant permits, matched to all eligible firms and excluding closures or internal reorganizations, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: No LMM 333618 qualifying-transfer cohort was found. Specialty engine makers may sell for succession or strategic consolidation, but certification, product liability, dealer support, warranty reserves, platform dependence, capital needs, technology-transition risk, and a narrow buyer set reduce the five-year probability.

principal_driver: Complex engineering, testing, calibration, and compliance workflows offer meaningful AI exposure around a durable installed base and backup-power applications.
principal_weakness: Electrification and integrated-OEM ownership may shrink both the addressable demand basket and the truly transferable LMM firm pool.
